summary: Timeboxed iterations divide project work into fixed periods where teams allocate user stories based on estimated capacity. The key discipline is that iteration end dates never slip - incomplete stories become new work items. Fowler recommends using 'Yesterday's Weather' to set capacity, specifically taking the minimum of recent iterations rather than the average to build in valuable slack. While continuous flow eliminates iteration overhead, iterations provide a rhythm and crucially make problems visible through their natural stopping function. Teams that regularly struggle to complete work or rush at iteration ends are seeing signals of underlying issues that slack time could address.
key_points: Iterations are strictly time-boxed with no mechanism to extend deadlines - incomplete work becomes new stories
Using the minimum of recent iterations for capacity planning (rather than average) builds in valuable slack
Continuous flow is simpler but iterations provide rhythm and problem visibility that many teams find valuable
The end of an iteration acts as a feedback loop that reveals estimation quality and team dysfunction
The cure for iteration-end struggles is adding more slack to address underlying issues, though this is sadly undervalued
money_quotes: Iterations are time-boxed, in that there is no mechanism to slip the end date of an iteration in order to complete any stories.
A better course is to use the minimum to provide valuable Slack. This mechanism is a feedback loop that naturally self-calibrates.
Iterations are most valuable due to their ability to make problems visible.
The end of an iteration is a natural stopping function that provides a check against serious overrun, setting up a feedback loop for the quality of estimates.
The best cure for these problems is to add more slack to work on the underlying issues, a cure that is sadly undervalued.
Although I'd like to think the soaring elegance of our Anglo-West-Coast prose is never to be surpassed in the genre, these days I would turn to The Art of Agile Development instead.
key_insight: The true value of timeboxed iterations isn't scheduling efficiency but their ability to make problems visible through a natural stopping function that forces teams to confront estimation failures and process dysfunction.
